sentences.base_language: Omar sends Layla a message in the afternoon.
sentences.target_language: عمر يرسل لليلى رسالة في المساء.
sentences.romanized: Omar yursil li-Layla risala fi al-masa'.
sentences.literal: Omar sends to-Layla message in the-evening.
sentences.grammarNote: يُرسِل (yursil, he sends) — Form IV of root ر-س-ل (r-s-l, to send/messenger). Form IV causative: أَرسَلَ (arsala, to send). رَسُول (rasūl, messenger/prophet — فَعُول pattern), رِسَالَة (risāla, message/letter — verbal noun). لِـ (li-, to/for) before a proper name introduces the indirect object: يُرسِل لِليلى = he sends to Layla.

sentences.base_language: Hi Layla! Are you free this evening?
sentences.target_language: مرحبا ليلى! هل أنتِ حرة الليلة؟
sentences.romanized: marhaba Layla! hal anti hurra al-layla?
sentences.literal: Hello Layla! Is you(f.) free the-tonight?
sentences.grammarNote: أنتِ (anti) is the feminine form of 'you'. Arabic has separate pronouns for masculine أنتَ (anta) and feminine أنتِ (anti). The adjective حرة (hurra, free) is also feminine to agree with Layla.

مَاذَا فِي الأَمر؟ (mādhā fī al-amr?) = what's the matter? — literally 'what is in the matter?' مَاذَا (mādhā) is used before a verb or prepositional phrase, while مَا is used before a noun. الأَمر (al-amr, the matter/affair) from root أ-م-ر (ʾ-m-r, to command/matter): أَمَرَ (to command), إِمَارَة (emirate), أَمِير (prince).

sentences.base_language: Do you want to meet for coffee?
sentences.target_language: هل تريدين أن نلتقي على القهوة؟
sentences.literal: Do you(f.)-want that we-meet over the-coffee?
sentences.grammarNote: تريدين (turidin) is the feminine second-person present verb — addressing a woman. The masculine would be تريد (turid). Arabic verbs conjugate differently for masculine and feminine subjects in the second person.

sentences.base_language: What a great idea! I love that.
sentences.grammarNote: فِكرَة رَائِعَة! (fikra rāʾiʿa!) — an exclamatory equational sentence with no verb: 'a wonderful idea!' يُعجِبُني (yuʿjibunī, it pleases me) — Form IV of root ع-ج-ب (ʿ-j-b, to be amazed/pleasing): أَعجَبَ (to please/amaze). The subject is هَذَا (this), and ني (nī) = me as object suffix. Arabic often uses 'it pleases me' where English says 'I love it.'

يُنَاسِبُك (yunāsibuki, it suits you — addressing a woman) — Form III of root ن-س-ب (n-s-b, to suit/be appropriate). Form III فَاعَلَ often implies interaction: نَاسَبَ (to be suitable for). The suffix كِ (-ki) = you (feminine). For a man: يُنَاسِبُك (-ka). أَيّ (ayy, which/what) + noun = which [noun]? — a very common question pattern.

sentences.base_language: How about five in the afternoon?
sentences.target_language: ما رأيك في الساعة الخامسة بعد الظهر؟
sentences.romanized: ma ra'yuki fi as-sa'a al-khamisa ba'd adh-dhuhr?
sentences.literal: What your(f.)-opinion in the-hour the-fifth after the-noon?
sentences.grammarNote: ما رأيك؟ (ma ra'yuk?) = What do you think? (lit. 'what is your opinion?'). رأي (ra'y) = opinion, with the possessive suffix ك (-k) = your. A very natural way to suggest something in Arabic.

sentences.base_language: Perfect! Where do you want to meet?
sentences.grammarNote: مُمتَاز (mumtāz, excellent) — Form VIII active participle from root م-ي-ز (m-y-z, to distinguish): اِمتَازَ (to stand out/be distinguished). نَلتَقِي (naltaqī, we meet) — Form VIII of root ل-ق-ي (l-q-y, to meet): اِلتَقَى (to meet each other). Form VIII of ل-ق-ي implies a mutual meeting. أَين (ayna, where) introduces a question about place.

sentences.base_language: There is a nice cafe near the park.
sentences.target_language: هناك مقهى جميل بالقرب من الحديقة.
sentences.romanized: hunak maqha jamil bil-qurb min al-hadiqa.
sentences.grammarNote: هُنَاك (hunāk, there is/there are) is an existential adverb used to introduce something new: هُنَاك مَقهى = there is a café. It functions like French 'il y a' or English 'there is.' مَقهى (maqhā, café) is a defective noun ending in ى — it does not take nunation (tanwin) in the indefinite. بِالقُرب مِن (bil-qurb min, near/close to) — a very common locative phrase: literally 'by the nearness of.'

sentences.base_language: I know that place! See you there.
sentences.grammarNote: أراك (araki — addressed to a woman) = I'll see you. It combines أرى (ara, I see) + the suffix كِ (-ki, you, f.). When saying 'see you' to a man: أراك (araka). The suffix changes with the gender of the person spoken to.
